claim: Standard sandboxing conflates execution isolation with access control; agents can exfiltrate or misuse API keys even in isolated containers, making them insecure by design
actionable: Don't rely solely on container-level sandboxing for agent security; implement network-level identity and access control to prevent credential exfiltration.
source_title: What if the network was the sandbox? — Remy Guercio, Tailscale

claim: WireGuard-based network identity (Tailscale) enables API key elimination: agents get network-verified identity instead of credentials, making exfiltration impossible
actionable: For agent sandboxing, use network-level identity protocols (WireGuard, Tailscale) instead of API keys; agents get verified identity, not credentials.
source_title: What if the network was the sandbox? — Remy Guercio, Tailscale

claim: Aperture (LLM gateway) captures every tool call, bash command, and MCP request at the network layer without container instrumentation; provides complete visibility and guaranteed enforcement
actionable: Use network-level LLM gateways to inspect and enforce constraints on tool calls; don't rely on harness instrumentation alone.
source_title: What if the network was the sandbox? — Remy Guercio, Tailscale

claim: Network-level gateways enable cross-provider cost control and quota management unified across Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, etc. without provider-specific interfaces
actionable: Deploy an LLM gateway that sits between agents and all LLM providers; enforce unified budgets, quotas, and cost controls across providers.
source_title: What if the network was the sandbox? — Remy Guercio, Tailscale

claim: Webhook integrations on gateways guarantee observability; every tool call must pass through hooks with no hiding or bypass possible
actionable: Set up webhooks on LLM gateways to log every tool call, model invocation, and cost event; use this for auditing, alerting, and debugging.
source_title: What if the network was the sandbox? — Remy Guercio, Tailscale
